summary: Stellar Red Rocks show with a thunder‑storm‑fueled first set and a blistering second set highlighted by Let It Grow, Shakedown and an epic St. Stephen tease; audience recording is surprisingly clear and lively.
review: The July 28 1982 Red Rocks run is repeatedly called one of the band’s all‑time greats – rain‑soaked first‑set energy gives way to a second set that fans rave about for Let It Grow, Shakedown, Althea and an unforgettable St. Stephen tease.  The audience (FOB) recording, captured by Jim Wise and other tapers, is praised as “fantastic”, “rich” and “well balanced” despite the usual crowd‑tape quirks, making this a highly listenable document of a legendary night.

ai_rating.rationale: Over 30 reviews overwhelmingly praise the performance (many 5‑star) and note only minor recording quirks; both show and tape quality score high, yielding an overall rating in the top tier.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Listeners highlight a lively ambience, clear instrument balance and good level control; occasional complaints about taper‑level adjustments but no major distortion or dropouts.

show_quality.setlist_flow: Rain‑soaked, high‑energy first set transitions into a second set that builds intensity; the band rides the storm’s mood into epic jams and tight song-to-song segues.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Repeatedly lauded for his tone, scorching solos on Let It Grow and Shakedown, and an especially humorous second‑solo that made listeners laugh.
band_member_comments.Phil: Mentioned as smiling and ready; no specific performance critique beyond general positivity.
band_member_comments.Bob: Praised for witty lyric changes (Truckin'), occasional forgetfulness of lyrics taken in good humor, and strong vocal delivery on NFA/Black Peter.
band_member_comments.Keys: Brent’s piano described as “jangly” and a highlight throughout the show; keyboards noted as contributing to the lush sound.
band_member_comments.Drums: The post‑drum jam after the first set singled out as “excellent”, with the drum break feeding into the St. Stephen tease.
